[PUBLISHED DATE]: 2017-05-24
[TITLE]: Were These Women Found Guilty of Witchcraft in Early 20th Century China? | Snopes.com
[METADATA/INTRO]: On 24 May 2017, the Historical Pics Twitter account posted a photograph purportedly showing three woman who were found guilty of witchcraft in China in 1922: This is a real photograph, but the women pictured were not convicted witches; nor...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: On 24 May 2017, the Historical Pics Twitter account posted a photograph purportedly showing three woman who were found guilty of witchcraft in China in 1922: 
 This is a real photograph, but the women pictured were not convicted witches; nor is the image from 1922. 
 Instead, the women posed for the photograph to illustrate the use of a three-person cangue, a public humiliation device. Photographer William Saunders took the picture sometime between 1870 and 1880 in Shanghai. The image is archived at the School of Oriental and African Studies in London. The photograph was also misdated as having been taken in 1907 in the book Imperial China Photographs. Régine Thiriez, who has written extensively about photography in 19th century China, said of the photograph:

[PUBLISHED DATE]: 2019-01-15
[TITLE]: Chinese Public Humiliation Stocks - Bondage Blog
[METADATA/INTRO]: Chinese Public Humiliation Stocks This is said to be a photo from 1875 of three women enduring a “minor” judicial punishment of public humiliation in China. These communal neck stocks are pretty commonly seen in drawings of historical Chinese punishments,...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: Chinese Public Humiliation Stocks 
 This is said to be a photo from 1875 of three women enduring a “minor” judicial punishment of public humiliation in China. These communal neck stocks are pretty commonly seen in drawings of historical Chinese punishments, but an actual photograph that shows women in the stocks is pretty rare. This is the first I’ve seen:

[PUBLISHED DATE]: Unknown Date
[TITLE]: Three women 'prisoners' posed in a cangue | Historical Photographs of China
[METADATA/INTRO]: Three women 'prisoners' posed in a cangue Collection...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: Three women 'prisoners' posed in a cangue 
 University of Bristol - Historical Photographs of China reference number: Os04-017. Photo from an album (UoB reference Os04) kept in the School of Oriental and African Studies Archives, London (SOAS reference MS 380 876/4). This photograph has been incorrectly dated at 1907 in many publications. This photograph has been set up (or posed) by the photographer, using models to illustrate the use of a three-person cangue. Photograph by William Saunders. See research by Régine Thiriez, on page 4 of this paper: http://pnclink.org/annual/annual1999/1999pdf/thiriez.pdf. See a similar set up/posed/illustrative photograph by William Saunders: https://uk.pinterest.com/pin/376965431290176132/. N.B. both of these often misinterpreted/misdated cangue photographs have the photographer's reference number inscribed on the lower panel of the door on the right - in Os04-017 this reference is hard to read.
